narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 35, you've worked hard to build your profile for law school, including planning to attend full-time and coming from a low family income background. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 63% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance lagged behind peers. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the average of 5.71, added to this concern by reinforcing a pattern of below-average grades in your school rankings. On a brighter note, your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA each pulled strongly in the opposite direction, suggesting potential for success closer to what the model sees in typical passers. Your low family income had a modest negative influence, as it sometimes correlates with added challenges in bar preparation, while your full-time status nudged things slightly toward passing, aligning with the vast majority of students who pursue that path. Compared to average applicants—who boast higher decile rankings, slightly better LSATs around 36.7, and GPAs near 3.21—your grades in school deciles stood out as the key shortfall driving the overall risk assessment. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA-enhancing prep, and exploring scholarships for your income situation. Many students in similar spots have improved their profiles and succeeded—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and reapply stronger. You've got this; persistence pays off in law. Keep pushing.
